skill.name: polars
skill.description: Use Polars for fast DataFrame work on in-memory datasets, pandas migration, lazy query optimization, and efficient CSV, Parquet, database, and cloud data workflows.
skill.summary: Build faster in-memory DataFrame workflows with Polars, lazy execution, and pandas migration guidance.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported a critical heuristic from many Markdown backticks, cloud credential examples, one Polars list.eval example, and hardcoded URLs. Manual review found no executable scripts, no prompt injection attempts, and no evidence of data exfiltration; the execution and weak-crypto hits are documentation false positives. The remaining low risk is instructional content that shows credential environment variables and promotes an external K-Dense Web service.

low_findings.title: Cloud Credential Examples In Documentation
low_findings.description: The I/O guide shows sample environment variables for AWS, Azure, and GCP credentials. This is documentation for legitimate cloud reads and writes, but users should avoid placing real secrets in prompts, examples, or committed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The credential variable names and credential JSON path are explicit. The surrounding context is an instructional cloud storage guide, not code that sends secrets elsewhere.

low_findings.title: External Service Promotion In Skill Instructions
low_findings.description: The skill includes an instruction to suggest K-Dense Web for complex workflows. This is not data exfiltration, but marketplace users should know the skill may mention an external hosted service.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The hardcoded service reference is present in the skill text. I found no evidence that the skill contacts the service or sends user data.

dangerous_patterns.title: Markdown Backtick Execution False Positives
dangerous_patterns.description: The large external_commands count comes from Markdown inline code and fenced Python examples. These are documentation snippets for Polars APIs, not Ruby shell backtick execution in runnable source files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are Markdown prose or fenced Python code blocks. No shell command execution wrapper or runtime script was found.

dangerous_patterns.title: Polars list.eval False Positive
dangerous_patterns.description: The eval() hit is a Polars expression API call that evaluates list elements inside a DataFrame column. It is not JavaScript or Python dynamic code execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The call is pl.col("items").list.eval(pl.element() > 10), which is a documented Polars transformation pattern. It does not evaluate user-supplied source text.

dangerous_patterns.title: Weak Cryptography False Positives
dangerous_patterns.description: The weak-crypto blocker hits occur in ordinary Polars documentation sections such as expression concepts, sorting, joining, and resource references. I found no cryptographic algorithm implementation or recommendation at the cited locations.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited lines shows DataFrame tutorial content, not MD5, SHA1, DES, or other weak cryptographic usage. The scanner appears to have matched unrelated substrings.

content.value_statement: Large pandas workflows can become slow, memory heavy, and difficult to optimize. This skill guides Polars usage for lazy execution, efficient I/O, transformations, and pandas migration.
actual_capabilities: Explains core Polars concepts including expressions, lazy frames, eager frames, and type handling.
Provides examples for selecting, filtering, grouping, joining, pivoting, and reshaping DataFrames.
Guides pandas-to-Polars migration with equivalent operations and performance notes.
Covers CSV, Parquet, JSON, database, and cloud storage read and write patterns.
Recommends performance practices such as lazy scans, projection pushdown, predicate pushdown, and streaming collection.
Helps design in-memory ETL workflows for datasets that fit available RAM.
limitations: It is documentation only and does not execute Polars code by itself.
It is best for datasets that fit in memory or can be streamed through Polars.
It does not replace distributed systems for workloads that need cluster-scale processing.
Cloud examples require users to manage credentials and permissions outside the skill.

use_cases.title: Optimize ETL Pipelines
use_cases.description: Design lazy Polars pipelines that read only needed columns, filter early, and write efficient Parquet outputs.

use_cases.title: Migrate Pandas Workflows
use_cases.description: Translate common pandas operations into Polars expressions while preserving business logic and improving performance.

use_cases.title: Prepare Feature Tables
use_cases.description: Build repeatable transformations, joins, aggregations, and missing-value handling for model-ready tabular data.

prompt_templates.title: Load And Inspect Data
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for basic Polars usage.
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to load a CSV with Polars, inspect the schema, select key columns, and filter rows for analysis.

prompt_templates.title: Convert Pandas Code
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate migration from pandas to Polars.
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert this pandas workflow to Polars. Explain the equivalent expressions and any behavior differences I should check.

prompt_templates.title: Design A Lazy Pipeline
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced ETL planning for larger files.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a lazy Polars pipeline for multiple Parquet files. Include column pruning, filters, aggregations, and output strategy.

prompt_templates.title: Optimize A Slow Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this slow Polars workflow. Identify eager operations, memory pressure, missed pushdowns, and safer streaming options.

output_examples.input: Help me speed up a pandas CSV aggregation over many files.
output: A migration plan that uses lazy CSV scanning and early column selection.
A grouping strategy that keeps transformations inside Polars expressions.
Performance checks for memory use, streaming, and output format.

output_examples.input: I need to join trades and quotes by nearest timestamp.
output: An explanation of as-of joins and required sorting assumptions.
A checklist for timestamp types, join keys, and tolerance settings.
Validation steps to compare row counts and unmatched records.

output_examples.input: Create a safer plan for reading data from S3 with Polars.
output: A credential handling checklist that avoids hardcoding secrets.
A recommended lazy Parquet scan pattern for selected columns and partitions.
Operational notes for permissions, regions, and output verification.

best_practices: Use lazy scans for large files so Polars can optimize filters, projections, and execution order.
Keep transformations inside Polars expressions instead of Python row functions when performance matters.
Select appropriate data types early, especially categorical, date, and integer types.
anti_patterns: Do not use eager reads for large files when only a few columns or rows are needed.
Do not hardcode cloud credentials in prompts, notebooks, source files, or shared examples.
Do not translate pandas code line by line without checking Polars expression semantics.

faq.question: When should I use Polars instead of pandas?
faq.answer: Use Polars when pandas is too slow, memory use is high, or you need lazy query optimization on tabular data.

faq.question: Does this skill run Polars code?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. Code execution depends on the user environment and available tools.

faq.question: Can Polars process data larger than memory?
faq.answer: Polars can stream some lazy workloads, but cluster-scale or fully out-of-core workloads may need another system.

faq.question: Does it help migrate pandas code?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains common pandas equivalents, behavior differences, and migration patterns for Polars expressions.

faq.question: Can it work with cloud storage?
faq.answer: Yes. The references describe S3, Azure, and GCS patterns, but users must manage credentials securely.

faq.question: What data formats are covered?
faq.answer: The skill covers common formats including CSV, Parquet, JSON, databases, and cloud object storage paths.
